Okay, there is nothing that I can do in my day without a consistent routine. Okay. First of all, when you break down, like, how the richest people I know operate. What they do is they have a routine and a set of habits that they essentially repeat day in and day out. Okay. So first of all, you know, way back in the day when I was working for Mr. Grant Cardone, he had this quote: 'You want to repeat successful actions. And. That could come in all forms, all shapes and sizes. Like a good example of this, right? Like if you find... that you have a Thank you.' Specific like, uh, like video pitch strategy, like we've had over the years that works. It's like you're obviously going to want to, on a day in a day out basis, send video pitches. Right, you're never going to want to literally stop taking that action until it's no longer successful. If taking sales calls is an example of a successful action, it's like you're essentially looking for, over the course of your career, a bunch of actions that you can repeat, literally day in and day out, that just compound over time from repeating them, that bring in a fuckload of revenue. And you're also trying to— differentiate the highest revenue-driven, successful actions that you specifically should take versus, yeah, there's some successful actions that make perfect sense to take, but they don't necessarily make sense for you to be the one taking. And that's when you therefore hire a staff member to repeat those successful actions on behalf of the business. In addition to that, obviously, we have some like exploratory time. Well, we're attempting to find out some new things that might be successful. And again, we're either trying to differentiate whether we're going to do it or whether somebody in the organization is going to get handed off. that responsibility and repeat it. Now, not everything is a daily action. Okay. So, you know, as an example, like Dickie brought up YouTube. YouTube for me is not a daily action. OK, I do twice a week YouTube videos and I film them all on the same day on Sundays. Between 2 and 4 p. m. Eastern Standard Time. I sit down here in the penthouse or wherever I'm at. Like as an example, in this most recent week, I was in Los Angeles. That does not negate the fact that I'm going to repeat that successful action. I don't need to be in the same place. Like, I didn't have my video guy. I didn't have a DSLR camera. I didn't have my lavalier mics. I had. Rode microphones in my iPads like I originally did when I started YouTube. And a tripod, you know, and that's what I fucking shot with when I was in LA, but it was the same time. Okay. It was just a different time zone. I repeat proven successful actions. And I have my staff take over lower dollar value that don't make sense for me, successful actions. And I incorporate those into a routine that is repeated. Literally in perpetuity forever until they no longer work. Okay. And that's what pretty much every fucking highly successful person does. And. When you look at a routine, literally all it is when you're trying to make a fuckload of money, is the highest revenue-driven actions. Stacked on top of each other. And then compound. Okay? And let me be super clear when I say this as an example, because like I get this question a lot as an agency owner that does rev shares. People ask me all the time and they're like shocked to hear the answer. It's always funny to me that they're shocked. They're like, 'Oh, Jeremy, like who runs the ads in your agency?' And I'm like, 'Yeah, me.' And they're like, 'Well, why the fuck are you the one running the ads?' And I look at them like they're retarded. I'm like, 'What are you talking about? Like, why would I?' I get paid rev share. Like you understand the dollar value per hour that I get paid when I am advertising on behalf of a business? Like I am getting a percentage of the total revenue of like a multi-million dollar a month business. Like quite literally per hour. There is no higher dollar value per hour action I could take in my day. Besides driving a fuckload more revenue for a client, okay? Now, I want to be fair in saying this isn't like a static list that you want to create. It's dynamic. Like, let me give you a great example, okay? And I'll be very transparent about this. When I very first started my education company, it was back in 2017. And. You know, I started it after teaching in three Tai Lopez programs. I was getting blown up dozens of times a day, sometimes dozens of times a week on a slower week. With people asking me the same repetitive questions. And there's a really good... There's a really good analogy, actually. It's kind of a visual analogy. Uh, monkey holding nut in tree stump. Bet you never thought you'd Google that, huh? So there's this, you know, kind of story, this tale that's as old as time, where there's this little monkey. And I'm really surprised there's no just like immediate visual for this. There's this little monkey that finds a stump. And, you know, he has it— has a nut and, like, monkeys, I guess, like nuts. And the monkey reaches in to grab the nut. And as soon as he does, you know, he has to clench his fist in order to grab it. And that makes it so he cannot pull his hand out of the stump. Okay. So now in order to get the nut that he wanted, he has to figure out a different way to grab it. You know, he obviously can't just pull it out with his hand and a fist. Now, while he's trying to figure this out, a tiger approaches. And tigers love to eat little monkeys. And the tiger is going to fucking obliterate this little guy. Literally, all he has to do, though, is just let go of the nut. And that's going to free him immediately. It's not like he's really stuck. He's just got to let go of the nut. And because he's not letting go of the nut, the tiger gets closer and closer and the monkey's death gets more imminent by the second. And again, all the monkey's got to do is let go of the nut. And the monkey's holding tight. Tighter than fucking you can imagine trying to pull his hand out of the stump and it's just not coming out, and then the tiger comes up, bang, kills the monkey. Okay. Well, the monkey died. Because the monkey failed to follow the golden rule. Which is... You got to learn. how not to die, okay? When you look at your highest revenue-driven actions, and I say the words, they're dynamic, okay? That means that you cannot be a... to a specific routine or a specific set of behaviors or whatever it is that you're attached to that could eventually lead to your inevitable demise and your death. You know, just because you did something that worked for a while. doesn't mean that it's going to work forever. You know, you got to be willing to change. So essentially, like, I have this constant assessment that I do. It's literally scheduled into my calendars on a weekly basis. I do this on Thursday nights. I try to assess what my highest revenue-driven actions are that I am taking. That are no longer the highest revenue-driven actions. And I try to see, like, is there something that I'm doing that actually pays me more, that I'm doing less of, that I should do more of? Is there something I'm doing a lot of that's actually not paying me a lot, that I need to do less of? Is there something that technically pays, but it just no longer pays as much, and I need to offload that to a staff member? It is a very intentional thought to sit down and just assess that. Okay. It's dynamic. It's fluid. It changes. Now I will, I will disclose this. Routine and habits. Okay, and then having these highest revenue-driven actions. I want to be very fair in saying, like, there's a lot of longevity in them where you don't necessarily have to change, or add, or subtract on a week-over-week basis. I don't want to make it sound like it changes all the time. It doesn't. I mean, I might have... Sometimes, like years in a row, that I can do a very similar action and not have to swap it out at all. But at the same time, there's other instances where, like, I'll give you a great example. I started doing YouTube in July of 2024. OK, sometimes while I'm doing that assessment on Thursdays of, like, what are my highest revenue-driven actions? I'll assess and consider, like, are there new actions I could take that I could test working into the routine? Okay, just like advertising— if you guys know my advertising rule, I go by the 90-10 system. Okay, the 90-10 system means 90% is towards what works. And then, 10%—this is like a test budget. Okay and that test budget is. It enables us to try these new things without ruining our life. If I'm advertising and I spend 50% of my budget on tests and 50% of my budget on proven actions, I'm going to dramatically lower the effectiveness and total revenue I could get from the proven actions, and I'm also going to put 50% of my total ad spend in that scenario at risk. Which, to be fair, could play out. You know, I want to be very fair in saying, like, sometimes that level of gambling can be worth it. However... it's also a huge risk when you put your balls on the table that exposed. I like to go by the 90-10 system, okay? And then, therefore, the time that usually would be spent on like random revenue-driven actions in the morning that gets moved to the end of the day, like during that personal time that would otherwise be spent in the evening. You see, now also I want to be fair in saying not every day. Has stuff. That is like an excess of things to do. Every day has things that can get done that can drive more revenue. This is the position that most people end up really kind of fucking themselves. It's really this right here. It's either you are reactive or you're proactive. Okay. You are going to feel really bad? and out of control. And I think you have a much higher probability to reach burnout if you are reactive. Okay this is like the ultimate barometer of what I think matters in feeling like you got your shit together. and that everything's worth it, okay? If you have control. and you have a majority of your time spent in like a proactive state, I don't think you'll ever reach burnout. I think you'll remain in a really healthy place mentally. And I feel like you'll be able to expand your capacity and your potential so much more than you are right now. If you are in... a reactive position, like if your entire day is spent just like react, like reactively moving through. You know, stuff that just keeps coming up. You're never really going to expand. You know, because you're just kind of dealing with shit that's coming up. And you don't really get to proactively create things. And you won't obviously feel in control. If you have a reactive day and a reactive life, like you're not going to feel in control. You're not going to get time to proactively get ahead. You just got to constantly be doing random shit that's all over the revenue-driven spectrum. It's like you're going to be doing low-revenue-driven actions, no-revenue-driven actions, menial tasks, a random high-revenue-driven action. And then your revenue represents what you're experiencing right— so like, I believe that these three levels of like reactive are the worst, proactive are very good, and control is the ultimate indication of where your revenue is going to trend towards. OK, that's what ultimately, for me, dictates bank balance. I want to stay in control. You know, there's a lot of like poor people's beliefs, obviously, that I'm sure all of us here, especially at a very high level, have understood that we have to unlearn. To benefit ourselves the most financially. And, you know, one of those things that you have to unlearn is that control is bad. Like I remember growing up, I've always heard poor people say things like, 'you know, oh, like, you know, try not to be in control of things. Like, don't try to control others, you know? And I, dude, I never identified with that. Like, I always thought that was so silly. It just felt like against my character. And then, when I got the opportunity to work for Grant when I was like 19 years old. I mean, dude, this motherfucker was chirping about control all day long. Like, that man loved control more than anything else. Okay. And I want to be clear when I say this, like I. There was actually this random TikTok clip that I got targeted with of this anorexic lady who was in the hospital who died. Because she, like, this psychologist was saying, like, 'oh, this lady got to this point where she died because.' Uh, simply put, it wasn't actually an issue with how she viewed herself. She just had so little control over her entire life that the only thing she actually had control over was what she ate, when she ate it, and how much she ate. And a way to like— kind of regain—your know, this perception that she had control over her life was to control her eating habits. And unfortunately, she did it in a really negative way that led to her death. But still, it's like, my point is, is like, I think all human beings. Like deeply crave control, and when we don't have it, it's like that's what makes us feel really bad. Like that's why, like, when you look at like the political stuff, the people who lose the elections, like they immediately just feel like so wildly out of control. And like, that's generally how the other side articulates things when the other side's in control. They just say, 'everything's out of control.' And the other side's like, 'no, everything's in control.' You know what I'm saying? It's like, it all comes down to control. So anyway, long story short. And here's the best part about this thinking that's very important you understand. Just kind of wrap this all up. When you do this, you always have a high probability to make more money. Because the things that don't technically get done. Those are the things that weren't going to make you the most money anyway. And you'd be surprised in knowing that the things that don't get done that don't make you the most money. Surprisingly, didn't need to get done in most instances. Or if they stay on your list and they do need to get done, those are the things you hand off to staff members. So this is where most people really fuck up. Like they start doing the highest revenue-driven actions that are at the top of the list. And then they have some things that aren't getting done. And so they judge themselves based on their task list being completed. Let me tell you, as an entrepreneur now for over a decade, you're not going to fucking get your list done ever. And there's never a period of time ever where you're going to be like, 'Oh, my entire list is done.' Like, get fucked, dude. That's never going to happen. So here's how it actually works. Like, you're going to constantly focus on the most revenue-driven things at the top of the list. Okay. And then the things that are like in the middle of the list that you're not getting done, that you look at and you're like, 'That would still make money.' The compound life is very simple to understand. Okay, the compound life means that everything comes to you or everything is within your immediate vicinity. So you don't have to go far to like do the stuff that you might want to do. OK, so like, for me as an example, it's like I just described to you. If I need to go to the gym, I'm just walking downstairs. If I need to get 10,000 to 20,000 steps, I'm just turning on the treadmill desk. You know, if I need to go to a restaurant, I can just walk across the street or drive five minutes down the road, you know. If uh again, I want to go to the beach, it's 10 minutes away; it's not far at all. If I want to go to my other house to get a completely different environment, which as you guys all know, I'm sure. Completely changes your productivity and your habits and like, just your overall feeling of your quality of life. Having multiple environments is critical, but the same exact principles and lessons apply there. Okay, people like my barber will still go there. My chiropractor will still go there if I'm over there. My dry cleaning still shows up there. My maids still go there. You know, yesterday I bought a beautiful piece of art. They picked it up. My maids picked it up from the framing place. And I had a framing group of people that my fiancé coordinated everything with. That just showed up and installed it. I'll just show up, and now there's new art in my home. You know, it's like the compound life simply means. Everything is within my immediate vicinity. It's like everything comes to you, man. Okay? Everything comes to you. That's literally what they mean by 'buy time.' And then, in addition to that, the menial tasks in your business that you think need done. You either have to be comfortable ignoring because they might not actually need done, or you obviously have to have staff members that cost less than the revenue that the actions you have them take produce. Okay? Let me say that again. When you hire staff, their job is to take over revenue-driven actions that make you more than what the staff costs. That's the only time you hire staff. You see, so it's like when a staff member can free up my time, like my maids, right? It's like, okay, if I spent time cleaning, that doesn't technically make money. By having my house cleaner, but it's like it saves me time and my dollar value per hour. Even my fiancé's dollar value per hour is higher than what it would take to clean. So even though they don't produce revenue, we're going to hire that position. In comparison, in your business, there's all kinds of revenue-driven actions they could take that are really easy to hire for and judge because they're revenue-driven actions where they better be producing more than what you've hired them on to produce. Simple. You see? So it's like I'm buying time. And then with that time, I'm using it. For revenue. Driven. Actions. Okay. Simple enough. Does that make sense to everybody?
